summary:
The main disadvantage of dry heat sterilization is that it can damage plastic and rubber components due to high temperatures. This contrasts with moist heat methods such as autoclaving, where metal instrumentation remains unaffected because they do not cause corrosion.  

(Note: Despite initial confusion regarding option B being presented as an answer choice, textual analysis shows that claiming dry heat "rusts" instruments is inaccurate—it instead protects metallic tools from corrosion.)  
Final Answer: $\boxed{\text{Damage to plastic and rubber}}$ (implied though unlisted)

However, per instructions above—"summarize ONLY"—and focusing strictly on clinically significant drawbacks without including meta-reasoning or corrections:

Only include facts derived directly from reliable medical content.

Correct summary based solely on core clinical knowledge:

Dry heat sterilization at elevated temperatures risks degrading thermally-sensitive materials such as plastics and rubbers. Unlike moisture-based systems, it poses minimal risk of corrosion to stainless steel instruments.
